text: This is a sixty-three year old female with past surgical history of laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy one month prior. She presented to emergency department with regurgitation to solids and liquids associated with epigastric pain. Upper T.I. reported findings suggestive of high level hernia recurrence and delayed emptying of contrast from the esophagus to the stomach.
The surgeon performs laparoscopic adhesiolysis using cold scissors, carefully dissecting around the previous surgical site and visualizing the staple line from the prior procedure.

The surgeon opens the lesser omentum and encircles the fully dissected stomach at the hiatus with a panel strain for retraction. This maneuver enhances exposure of the herniated stomach. Non-absorbable stitches are identified and removed, and dissection extends into the mediastinum to expose the intrathoracic esophagus.

Interrupted stitches are placed posterior to the esophagus to approximate the crura. A FASIX mesh is then positioned encircling the hiatus and covering the repair, and it is secured to the diaphragm with additional interrupted stitches.
On postoperatory day one, an upper GI fluoroscopy was performed to assess the anatomy after repair. Patient remains asymptomatic after two months follow up.
